In recent years, a research team developed a  $[3 + 2 + 1]$  strategy for synthesizing quinoline rings, with the model reaction as follows:

The image shows an organic synthesis flowchart. The process is described as follows: First, two molecules of  $\mathrm{Ph}_3\mathrm{P} = \mathrm{O}$  and one molecule of  $\mathrm{Tf}_2\mathrm{O}$  form A. A reacts with an organic molecule (SMILES:

C1=CC=C(C=C1)NC(=O)[R1]) to generate intermediate B, during which A is converted to C. Intermediate B then reacts with an organic molecule (SMILES: C(#C[R2])[R2]) to produce the final product shown on the far right, with the SMILES formula C1=CC=C2C(=C1)C(=C(C(=N2)[R1])[R2])[R2].

The reaction below utilizes this model reaction to synthesize quinuclidine:

The image shows an organic synthesis roadmap. The synthetic route is described as follows: The starting reactant on the left (SMILES: CC1=C(C=CC=C1C#N)C#N) forms D under the conditions

$\mathrm{t - BuOK + (COOEt)_2}$ .  $\mathbf{D}$  reacts with  $\mathrm{HCCCH}_2\mathrm{OH}$  to form  $\mathbf{E}$  under the conditions  $\mathrm{PBu}_3$ , DEAD.

$\mathbf{E}$  is converted to  $\mathbf{F}$  under the conditions of first adding LiOH, then  $(\mathrm{COCl})_2$  and finally  $\mathrm{PhNH}_2$ .  $\mathbf{F}$  is converted to  $\mathbf{G}$  under the conditions  $\mathrm{Ph}_3\mathrm{P} = \mathrm{O}$ ,  $\mathrm{Tf}_2\mathrm{O}$ .

Regarding the nine unknown species  $\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{G}$  in the two figures above, the known information is: Intermediate  $\mathbf{B}$  is positively charged and contains no oxygen atoms.

The correct options are:

A. The electrophilicity of intermediate B is very poor.  
B. The predominant form of  $\mathbf{D}$  in aqueous solution does not contain hydroxyl groups  
C. The product  $\mathbf{G}$  contains three rings  
D. The molecular formula of  $\mathbf{G}$  is  $\mathrm{C_{20}H_{10}N_3O}$ .  
E. G undergoes complete hydrolysis under acidic conditions, and the products contain four carbonyl groups.  
F. G contains a five-membered ring that is unstable under acidic conditions.

Correct Answer: F

### Detailed Explanation
First, analyze the model reaction. The reactants are 2 equivalents of triphenylphosphine oxide and 1 equivalent of trifluoromethanesulfonic anhydride.  $\mathrm{Tf}_2\mathrm{O}$ , as a strong electrophile and dehydrating agent, reacts with triphenylphosphine oxide to generate a phosphonium salt, specifically the bis(triphenylphosphine oxide) salt, commonly referred to as an analog of the Mukaiyama reagent. Its structure is  $[(Ph_3P)_2O]^{2+}(TfO^-)_2$ , denoted as A. This is a highly potent oxygen-atom-activating reagent.

### Checkpoints Analysis
1 PTS

The structure of  $\mathbf{A}$  is  $\left[(\mathrm{Ph}_3\mathrm{P})_2\mathrm{O}\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{TfO}^-)_2$

The substrate N-acylaniline reacts with reagent A. The carbonyl oxygen of the amide acts as a nucleophile to attack one of the phosphorus atoms in A. Subsequently, the lone pair on the nitrogen atom can delocalize toward the carbonyl carbon, expelling a triphenylphosphine oxide and yielding a positively charged nitrilium ion with the structure  $\mathrm{R}_1 - \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}^+ - \mathrm{Ph}$ , which satisfies the property of being devoid of oxygen. Thus, this intermediate is B. Due to its strong electrophilicity, option A is incorrect.

1 PTS

The structure of  $\mathbf{B}$  is  $\mathrm{R}_1 - \mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}^+ - \mathrm{Ph}$

Next, analyze the synthetic route. The starting material is 2,3-dicyanotoluene. Potassium tert-butoxide (t-BuOK), a strong base, abstracts a proton from the benzylic position, generating a stable benzylic anion.

1 PTS

t-BuOK abstracts a proton from the benzylic position

This anion acts as a nucleophile, undergoing nucleophilic addition to one of the carbonyl groups of diethyl oxalate to form a  $\beta$ -diketone structure, denoted as D. Its SMILES is CCOC(=O)/C(=C/C1=C(C=CC=C1C#N)C#N)/O. Due to the presence of an intramolecular hydrogen bond in the six-membered ring, the enol form predominates, making option B incorrect.

1 PTS

D is a  $\beta$ -diketone structure, predominantly in the enol form, with SMILES CCOC(=O)/C(=C/C1=C(C=CC=C1C#N)C#N)/O

The formation of  $\mathbf{E}$  involves the famous Mitsunobu reaction, where the enolic hydroxyl group of  $\mathbf{D}$  condenses with propargyl alcohol, dehydrating to form an ether linkage. The net result is the replacement of the enolic hydroxyl hydrogen with a propargyl group. The SMILES structure is C#CCO/C(=C\C1=C(C=CC=C1C#N)C#N)/C(=O)OCC.

1 PTS

$\mathbf{E}$  is formed by replacing the enolic hydroxyl hydrogen of  $\mathbf{D}$  with a propargyl group, with SMILES C#CCO/C(=C\C1=C(C=CC=C1C#N)C#N)/C(=O)OCC

The reaction to form  $\mathbf{F}$  begins with the addition of lithium hydroxide, hydrolyzing the ethyl ester group derived from diethyl oxalate to a carboxylic acid. Subsequent treatment with oxalyl chloride converts the carboxylic acid to an acyl chloride, which is then reacted with aniline to form an amide. The final result is the conversion of the ethyl ester group to an N-phenylamide. The SMILES structure is C#CCO/C(=C\C1=C(C=CC=C1C#N)C#N)/C(=O)N[Ph].

1 PTS

$\mathbf{F}$  is formed by converting the ethyl ester group of  $\mathbf{E}$  to an N-phenylamide, with SMILES C#CCO/C(=C\C1=C(C=CC=C1C#N)C#N)/C(=O)N[Ph]

The final step involves the application of the model reaction, where the N-phenylamide is converted to a nitrilium ion. Due to the presence of an alkyne group within the molecule, an intramolecular model reaction occurs, forming a quinoline ring followed by aromatization. The final product  $\mathbf{G}$  has the SMILES structure C1=CC2=CC3=C(/C(=C\C4=C(C=CC=C4C#N)C#N)/OC3)N=C2C=C1.

2 PTS

The SMILES structure of  $\mathbf{G}$  is  $C1 = C2 = C3 = C(/C(= C\backslash C4 = C(C = CC = C4C\# N)C\# N) / OC3)N = C2C = C1$

Based on this structure: its molecular formula is  $\mathrm{C_{20}H_{11}N_3O}$ , containing four rings, among which the five-membered ring is a hemiketal structure. Under acidic conditions, it readily undergoes ring-opening to form an alcohol and a ketone, rendering it unstable.

1 PTS

The five-membered ring in  $\mathbf{G}$  is a hemiketal structure, which readily undergoes ring-opening under acidic conditions to form an alcohol and a ketone

Under acidic hydrolysis, the five-membered ring opens to form a ketone carbonyl group, while the two cyano groups hydrolyze to form two carboxylic carbonyl groups, totaling three carbonyl groups. Thus, options C, D, and E are incorrect, and option F is correct.

### Detailed Explanation
From the reaction ratios given in the table, it can be seen that product B is formed upon addition of 1 eq of iodine, and product C is formed upon further addition of 1 eq of iodine. Therefore, the reaction mainly consists of two steps, and the formation of product C requires more than 1 eq of iodine.

Product  $\mathbf{C}$  is not formed when only 1 eq of iodine is added

$$
C = C C N (S (C 1 = C C = C (C) C = C 1) (= O) = O) C C 2 = C ([ I + ] 2) C 3 = C C = C 3 N (C) C
$$

$$

Finally, a reaction occurs in which iodide attacks the methyl group, yielding product B:

C=CCN(S(C1=CC=C(C)C=C1)(=O)=O)CC2=C(I)C3=CC=CC=C3N2C

It is known that  $\mathrm{[XeF][AsF_6]}$  reacts with  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  in anhydrous HF according to the following equilibrium:

$$
\left[ \mathrm {X e F} \right]\left[ \mathrm {A s F} _ {6} \right] + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {H F} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {X e F} _ {2} + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} \right]\left[ \mathrm {A s F} _ {6} \right]
$$

When equimolar amounts of  $\mathrm{[XeF][AsF_6]}$  and  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  react in anhydrous HF at  $-78^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the adduct  $\mathrm{[H_3O][AsF_6]}\cdot 2\mathrm{XeF}_2$  is initially formed. Subsequently,  $\mathrm{[XeF][AsF_6]}$  reacts with free  $\mathrm{XeF}_2$  in a 1:1 ratio to yield compound A. This adduct further reacts with free  $\mathrm{XeF}_2$  to form a 1:1 salt B. The anion of B is identical to that of A, while its cation contains oxygen with a coordination number of 2. The mass fraction of Xe in B is approximately  $60\%$ . The cation of B is obtained from the cation of A by reacting with the electrically neutral species C, a process involving a single proton transfer.

Which of the following statements is correct:

A. The cation of  $\mathbf{A}$  is linear in shape.  
B. The relative molecular mass of A is smaller than that of C.  
C. The cations of  $\mathbf{B}$  are triangular in shape.  
D. The chemical reaction equation for the formation of  $\mathbf{B}$  (with coefficients in the simplest whole-number ratio), where the sum of the coefficients on the right side of the equation is 3.  
E. C contains 3 types of elements.  
F. The number of atoms contained in C is 4

Correct Answer: F

### Detailed Explanation
The formation of  $\mathbf{A}$  is straightforward. Since it is a 1:1 reaction,  $\mathrm{XeF_2}$  can only insert into  $\mathrm{XeF^{+}}$  to yield  $\mathrm{Xe}_2\mathrm{F}_3^+$ , which is the cation of  $\mathbf{A}$ . According to VSEPR theory, one F atom bridges the two Xe atoms and adopts  $\mathrm{sp}^3$  hybridization, resulting in a trigonal shape rather than a linear geometry. Therefore, option A is incorrect.

### Checkpoints Analysis
2 PTS

The cation of  $\mathbf{A}$  is  $\mathrm{Xe}_2\mathrm{F}_3^+$  with a trigonal shape

The anion of  $\mathbf{B}$  is the same as that of  $\mathbf{A}$ , both being hexafluoroarsenate anions. The cation contains oxygen with a coordination number of 2, suggesting it is composed of  $\mathrm{Xe}$ ,  $\mathrm{O}$ , and  $\mathrm{F}$ . Combining the mass fraction, the chemical formula of  $\mathbf{B}$ 's cation is determined to be  $\mathrm{Xe}_3\mathrm{OF}_3^+$ .

2 PTS

The chemical formula of B's cation is  $\mathrm{Xe}_3\mathrm{OF}_3^+$

Considering its structure, oxygen cannot be a terminal atom and must act as a bridging atom. Based on the structure of  $\mathrm{Xe}_2\mathrm{F}_3^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Xe}_3\mathrm{OF}_3^+$  can be derived by replacing a terminal fluorine atom in  $\mathrm{Xe}_2\mathrm{F}_3^+$  with an oxygen atom and then attaching a XeF unit, described as F - Xe - F - Xe - O - Xe - F. According to hybridization theory, it adopts a bent shape, making option C incorrect.

1 PTS

$\mathrm{Xe}_3\mathrm{OF}_3^+$  has a bent shape

Thus, the reaction for forming  $\mathbf{B}$  can be written as:

$$
3 \mathrm {X e F} _ {2} + [ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ] [ \mathrm {A s F} _ {6} ] = [ \mathrm {X e} _ {3} \mathrm {O F} _ {3} ^ {+} ] [ \mathrm {A s F} _ {6} ] + 3 \mathrm {H F}
$$

The sum of the coefficients on the right side of the equation is 4, so option D is incorrect.

$\mathrm{Xe}_3\mathrm{OF}_3^+$  is generated from  $\mathrm{Xe}_2\mathrm{F}_3^+$  and  $\mathbf{C}$  via a proton transfer process. Given that  $\mathrm{Xe}_3\mathrm{OF}_3^+$  is equivalent to replacing a terminal fluorine atom in  $\mathrm{Xe}_2\mathrm{F}_3^+$  with an oxygen atom and attaching a XeF unit, the displaced fluorine atom likely combines with the proton. Therefore, the structure of  $\mathbf{C}$  is clearly HOXeF, containing four elements and four atoms. Thus, option F is correct, while options E and C are incorrect.

1 PTS

The structure of  $\mathbf{C}$  is HOXeF

The FeSe-based system is the simplest type of iron-based superconducting material. The filling of iron vacancies with Cu in the form of a univalent cation will affect the structure and electrical and magnetic properties of the superconductor. In this experiment, precipitation gravimetry and EDTA complexometric titration were used to determine the chemical composition of a Fe - Cu - Se superconductor. The specific experimental procedure is as follows:

(a) Accurately weigh approximately  $0.4639\mathrm{g}$  of the sample into a  $100\mathrm{mL}$  beaker, add  $6\mathrm{mL}$  of concentrated nitric acid and  $6\mathrm{mL}$  of concentrated hydrochloric acid, and heat. Add concentrated hydrochloric acid during heating until the sample is completely dissolved. Add  $20\%$  hydroxylamine hydrochloride solution dropwise under stirring and heating until precipitation is complete, keep warm for a period of time, carefully filter out the precipitate with pre-weighed filter paper, wash several times with deionized water, dry at low temperature until constant weight, and record the mass as  $0.2565\mathrm{g}$ .  
(b) Weigh approximately  $0.8527\mathrm{g}$  of the sample into a  $100~\mathrm{mL}$  beaker, add  $10~\mathrm{mL}$  of concentrated nitric acid and  $10~\mathrm{mL}$  of concentrated hydrochloric acid, and heat. Add concentrated hydrochloric acid during heating until the sample is completely dissolved. Concentrate the solution to about  $10~\mathrm{mL}$ , and dilute to  $100~\mathrm{mL}$  with deionized water. Transfer  $25.00~\mathrm{mL}$  of the above solution to a conical flask, and adjust the  $\mathsf{pH}$  to  $3\sim 4$  with  $1:1$  ammonia water; add  $5\mathrm{g}$  of solid ammonium fluoride, shake to dissolve, then add  $5\mathrm{mL}$  of  $2\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}$  acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer solution, 5 drops of PAN indicator, and titrate slowly with  $0.02035\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}$  EDTA until the endpoint is reached when the purple-red color changes to gray-blue-green, consuming  $13.94~\mathrm{mL}$ .  
(c) Transfer  $10.00 \, \mathrm{mL}$  of the  $100 \, \mathrm{mL}$  solution prepared in (b) to a  $100 \, \mathrm{mL}$  volumetric flask and dilute to the mark with distilled water. Transfer  $25.00 \, \mathrm{mL}$  of the solution to a small beaker, add  $7 \, \mathrm{mL}$  of  $1:1$  ammonia water while stirring, heat for a period of time, and filter under normal pressure after the precipitate is obviously agglomerated. Wash the precipitate three times with deionized water. Discard the filtrate. Dissolve the precipitate with  $15 \, \mathrm{mL}$  of  $0.4 \, \mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}$  sulfuric acid, collect the solution in a conical flask, add  $10 \, \mathrm{mL}$  of deionized water, 5 drops of sulfosalicylic acid indicator, and titrate with  $0.008140 \, \mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}$  EDTA until the purple-red color fades significantly, which is the endpoint, consuming  $14.53 \, \mathrm{mL}$ .

For this experiment, the following options are correct (for options involving calculations, consider errors within  $0.1\%$  as correct)

A. Ascorbic acid thiourea can be used to mask Cu to measure Fe  
B. Cyanide masking of Cu can be used to measure Fe.  
C. Sulfur ion masking of Cu can be used to measure Fe.  
D. Hydrochloric acid + hydrogen peroxide can be used as a complete replacement for nitric acid + hydrochloric acid.  
E. (a) In experiments, high-temperature drying can be used to speed up the precipitation process.  
F. (a) If there is no holding period at a constant temperature during the experiment, the calculated mass fraction of Se will be higher.  
G. (c) The purpose of adding ammonia in the experiment is to adjust the  $\mathrm{pH}$  to slightly acidic.  
H. (c) The titration temperature in the experiment was room temperature.  
1. Representing the alloy with  $\mathrm{Fe}_x\mathrm{Cu}_y\mathrm{Se}$ , then  $x + y = 0.8969$  
J. The ratio of ferrous iron to ferric iron is approximately 2.516 : 1 .  
K. The mass fraction of Se is 1.954 times that of Fe.  
L. All of the above options are incorrect.

Correct Answer: J

### Detailed Explanation
Ascorbic acid thiourea, cyanide ions, and sulfide ions masking Cu will all react with  $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ , so they cannot be used, therefore options A, B, and C are all incorrect.

### Checkpoints Analysis
1 PTS

Because ascorbic acid thiourea, cyanide ions, and sulfide ions all react with iron, options A, B, and C are all incorrect

Hydrogen peroxide decomposes upon heating, and  $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$  catalyzes the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide, consuming too much hydrogen peroxide; and a small amount of nitric acid must be added to completely dissolve the sample, so it cannot be replaced, option D is incorrect.

1 PTS

Because hydrogen peroxide is catalyzed by iron and decomposes upon heating, and nitric acid needs to be added to dissolve the sample, option D is incorrect

(a) In the experiment, high-temperature drying of the precipitate will cause Se to sublime, resulting in less precipitate obtained, option E is incorrect.

1 PTS

High-temperature drying of the precipitate causes selenium to sublime, losing precipitate, option E is incorrect

(a) In the experiment, if it is not kept warm for a period of time, the precipitate will not be sufficiently aged, resulting in less precipitate obtained, and the calculated mass fraction of Se will be lower, option F is incorrect.

0.5 PTS
